The analysis examines the marginal effect of an additional dry spell day, considering combinations of minimum durations for dry spells and heat waves. Results are based on fixed effects regression analysis for different scenarios; 2000 is the midpoint of the study period.
Negative impact of dry spell on crop production in the current year. Value is scaled from proportion to percentage.
Positive impact of dry spell on crop production with a 1-year lag. Value is scaled from proportion to percentage.
Positive impact of dry spell on crop production with a 2-year lag. Value is scaled from proportion to percentage.
Positive impact of dry spell on crop production with a 3-year lag. Value is scaled from proportion to percentage.
Negative impact of dry spell on crop production with a 4-year lag. Value is scaled from proportion to percentage.
Positive impact of dry spell on crop production with a 5-year lag. Value is scaled from proportion to percentage.

The outcome is the percentage change in aggregate crop production due to an additional dry spell day, estimated over the period from 1979-2016. The standard error is reported alongside the coefficient. The impact is specified under several models (1)-(11) in Table A3, showing the robustness of the negative impact of dry spell days.  Year is set as 2000 to reflect the approximate midpoint of the study period.

The result refers to the controlled direct effect of an additional dry spell day with a minimum duration of 28+ days, while considering the influence of heat wave days and their interaction with dry spells.
The analysis reports the effect of an additional dry spell day impact on growth during different time periods, including the historically hottest 90 days.
Includes analysis for the hottest 120 days within a grid cell.
Includes analysis for a full year period, without specific temporal restriction.
The scenarios were weighted by crop area using MIRCA2000 data. This scenario considers the hottest 90 days, weighted.
